title: Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Excel   | Stats NZ
content:
## Using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API in Excel
16 October 2025, 11:00am
This step-by-step guide shows you how to retrieve data in Excel using the Aotearoa Data Explorer API.

## Step 1: Get an API key
Sign up to Stats NZ’s API Portal if you haven’t already. In the portal, subscribe to the Aotearoa Data Explorer API and generate a primary key. You’ll need this key in step 5.
API Portal explains how to sign up and create and manage subscription keys.

## Step 2: Determine the URL for the data you want
The data you want needs to be defined in a URL. You’ll need this in step 5.
An easy way to determine a URL is to use the Aotearoa Data Explorer browser to find and customise a table. Then select the Developer API option above the table (2a in the screenshot below) and copy the data query URL (2b).
Note that Excel has a display limit of 1 million rows, and the API will deliver data in a format that has one data point per row. If the URL you use requests more than 1 million data points, they won’t all show in Excel.
Aotearoa Data Explorer – API user guide has more detail about URLs.

## Step 3: Select the Data menu
In Excel, select the Data menu item.

## Step 4: Choose to import data from the web
Choose to get data From Web . This may show in the Get & Transform Data options. Otherwise, open the Get Data drop-down, select From Other Sources , then From Web .

## Step 5: Enter API details
On the screen shown:
- in the first URL parts box, enter the URL for the data query (from step 2)
- in the second URL parts box, enter “&format=csvfilewithlabels” to set the format as CSV.
- in HTTP request header parameters , type: “Ocp-Apim-Subscription-Key” to set the header for the API key
- enter your primary key (from step 1)
- click the OK button once all details have been entered.

## Step 6: If you get an error
The first time you set this up in Excel, you may get an error. If this happens, choose Anonymous . Once this is set up, you should not see this warning again.

## Step 7: Confirm the format
The screen below should pop up in Excel. Right-click the file and select CSV (if that was the format you specified in step 5c).

## Step 8: Finish and apply your query
You should see the data loaded into your screen and can apply further transformations, for example, remove columns you don’t need. Choose Close & Load to return to the Excel workspace.
The data should now be ready for use in Excel.
